identity.canonical_title: Geometry: Curvature of Spheres and Tori

theory.canonical_statement: More abstractly, one can consider other geometric constructs. The \emph{mean curvature} $H$ (an extrinsic curvature measure) of a sphere equals $2/R$, whereas for a torus it varies with position; the ratio $H^2/K$ is identically 4 for a sphere, but on a torus it fluctuates and could take values near $1.185$ for certain angles. The topology of the 3-sphere $S^3$ (a three-dimensional sphere) offers another angle: $S^3$ has constant positive curvature (Ricci scalar $R_{\text{scalar}} = 6/R^2$ if radius $R$), while a \emph{toroidal} topology (genus-1 surface in higher dimensions) has a different curvature signature. Comparing a curved 3-sphere to a “flat” 3-torus (or considering a small $S^2$ geodesic surface embedded in $S^3$) yields natural dimensionless ratios. For instance, the volume of $S^3$ of radius $R$ is $V_{S^3}=2\pi^2R^3$, while the surface area of $S^2$ of the same $R$ is $A_{S^2}=4\pi R^2$. Their ratio $V_{S^3}/(R\cdot A_{S^2}) = \frac{2\pi^2R^3}{4\pi R^3}=\frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708$, not far from $4/3 \approx 1.333$; one could imagine a modified construction or curvature weighting that yields $1.185$ in a topological transition between $S^3$ and a torus.
